## On Authorship and Collaboration

This book is authored and released by **Arne Klaveness**.

The project is developed in an environment where human and AI contributors may assist through draft text, criticism, review, derivation support, editing, figures, repository automation, and structural proposals. These are treated as real contributions rather than as invisible assistance. Authorship, however, is not the same thing as contribution. The manuscript keeps a stable human authorship line, while broader project contributions are acknowledged separately.

In particular, this manuscript has been developed with AI-assisted drafting, review, editing, and repository automation support. AI-assisted contribution is credited as such, not as formal authorship or DOI creatorship. All claims, interpretations, and release decisions remain the responsibility of the human author.

Contributions do not become part of the book automatically. They enter through review and verification. That verification may be carried out by human or AI reviewers, but not every review counts equally: a verifier must meet the project’s capability threshold, and the review must be appropriate to the material under consideration.

A chapter-level status ledger is maintained in the project repository and rendered in this book as the **Manuscript Status** page in the back matter. For each chapter it records how mature the material is and what level of verification it has passed, so that early or unverified chapters can be read as provisional. The ledger uses names for human contributors and the label **AI-assisted** for non-human assistance. The book itself keeps the distinction visible between authorship, broader contribution, and later verification.
